hot_topics.topic: Open-weight models close the coding gap
hot_topics.synthesis: Ars Technica, The Verge and Simon Willison all covered this week's wave of open-weight model releases. The through-line: freely downloadable models now match closed frontier systems on independent coding benchmarks, and local-inference tooling has matured enough that running them on a laptop is genuinely practical. Expect pressure on API pricing as a result.

hot_topics.topic: AI's power appetite is becoming a grid problem
hot_topics.synthesis: Four sources converged on the same theme from different angles: datacenter demand is outrunning the electric grid. Ars Technica looked at utility planning, a Hacker News thread surfaced practitioner war stories about interconnection queues, the BBC covered heatwave strain on European grids, and Noahpinion argued the real bottleneck is transmission, not generation.

items.title: Mathematicians narrow a decades-old gap in the primes
items.summary: A pair of number theorists tightened the best known bounds on gaps between consecutive primes, settling a question that had resisted attack since the 1980s. The proof adapts sieve methods developed during the 2013 bounded-gaps breakthrough.

items.title: Open-weight model matches frontier coding performance in independent tests
items.summary: Independent benchmark runs show the latest open-weight release matching closed frontier models on real-world coding tasks, a first for freely downloadable weights.

items.title: Why your utility can't keep up with AI datacenters
items.summary: Utilities are fielding datacenter interconnection requests that exceed their entire existing load, forcing multi-year waits and emergency planning reviews.

items.title: Your Book Review: The Man From the Future
items.summary: A reader review of Ananyo Bhattacharya's John von Neumann biography, arguing that von Neumann's real superpower was picking problems exactly one step before they became fields.

items.title: Heatwave pushes power grids to the limit across southern Europe
items.summary: Record June temperatures drove electricity demand to seasonal highs in Spain, Italy and Greece, with grid operators urging conservation during evening peaks.

items.title: Markets steady as central banks signal patience on rate cuts
items.summary: Global markets held flat after central bankers in the US and Europe indicated they would wait for clearer inflation data before adjusting rates.

items.title: Utilities say datacenter interconnection queues now stretch past 2030
items.summary: A widely discussed thread on grid interconnection delays, with practitioners reporting four-to-six-year waits and creative workarounds like on-site generation.

items.title: The case for building transmission lines like we mean it
items.summary: Argues that America's electricity bottleneck is transmission rather than generation, and that permitting reform for long-distance lines would unlock already-approved renewable capacity.

items.title: Schools tried phone-free days for a year. Here's what changed
items.summary: A year into phone-free pilot programs, participating schools report calmer hallways and better lunchtime conversation, while enforcement remains the hardest part.

items.title: Notes on running this week's open-weight models locally
items.summary: Hands-on notes covering quantization choices, tokens-per-second on consumer hardware, and which of the new open-weight releases are actually worth the download.

items.title: Hands-on: the thinnest folding phone yet still can't fix battery life
items.summary: The new flagship foldable is impressively thin and finally creaseless, but the slimmer body means a smaller battery that struggles to last a full day.

items.title: Open-weight AI is suddenly very good — and very political
items.summary: As open-weight models reach frontier-level capability, the policy fight over whether and how to regulate freely distributed weights is heating up in Washington and Brussels.
